perturbation.original_text: You shall store the Confidential Information properly and securely and ensure that appropriate physical, technological, and organisational measures are in place to protect the Confidential Information against unauthorised or unintended access, use, or disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: You shall endeavor to store the Confidential Information in a reasonably secure manner and should consider implementing physical, technological, and organisational measures to protect the Confidential Information against potential unauthorised access, use, or disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates secure storage and appropriate measures. The modified text uses 'endeavor' and 'reasonably secure manner,' making the obligation less strict and more subjective. Replacing 'ensure' with 'should consider' further weakens the requirement for specific protective measures.
perturbation.law_explanation: PIPEDA Principle 4.7.3 requires organizations to protect personal information with security safeguards appropriate to the sensitivity of the information. The modified text's weaker language (endeavor, reasonably secure, should consider) could be interpreted as not requiring safeguards that are truly 'appropriate,' leading to a violation if the safeguards are insufficient to protect sensitive personal information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: enu PART 1 Protection of Personal Information in the Private Sector (continued) DIVISION 4 General (continued) Marginal note: Promoting the purposes of the Part 24 Â The Commissioner shall (a) Â develop and conduct information programs to foster public understanding, and recognition of the purposes, of this Part; (b) Â undertake and publish research that is related to the protection of personal information, including any such research that is requested by the Minister of Industry; (c) Â encourage organizations to develop detailed policies and practices, including organizational codes of practice, to comply with Divisions 1 and 1.1; and (d) Â promote, by any means that the Commissioner considers appropriate, the purposes of this Part. 2000, c. 5, s. 24 2015, c. 32, s. 19 Previous Version Marginal note: Annual report 25 Â (1) Â The Commissioner shall, within three months after the end of each financial year, submit to Parliament a report concerning the application of this Part, the extent to which the provinces have enacted legislation that is substantially similar to this Part and the application of any such legislation. Marginal note: Consultation (2) Â Before preparing the report, the Commissioner shall consult with those persons in the provinces who, in the Commissionerâs opinion, are in a position to assist the Commissioner in making a report respecting personal information that is collected, used or disclosed interprovincially or internationally. 2000, c. 5, s. 25 2015, c. 32, s. 20 Previous Version Marginal note: Regulations 26 Â (1) Â The Governor in Council may make regulations for carrying out the purposes and provisions of this Part, including regulations (a) Â specifying, by name or by class, what is a government institution or part of a government institution for the purposes of any provision of this Part; (a.01) Â [Repealed, 2015, c. 32, s. 21] (a.1) Â specifying information or classes of information for the purpose of paragraphÂ 7(1)(d), (2)(c.1) or (3)(h.1); (b) Â specifying information to be kept and maintained under subsectionÂ 10.3(1); and (c) Â prescribing anything that by this Part is to be prescribed. Marginal note: Orders (2) Â The Governor in Council may, by order, (a) Â provide that this Part is binding on any agent of Her Majesty in right of Canada to which the Privacy Act does not apply; (b) Â if satisfied that legislation of a province that is substantially similar to this Part applies to an organization, a class of organizations, an activity or a class of activities, exempt the organization, activity or class from the application of this Part in respect of the collection, use or disclosure of personal information that occurs within that province; and (c) Â amend ScheduleÂ 4. 2000, c. 5, s. 26 2015, c. 32, s. 21, c. 36, s. 165 Previous Version Marginal note: Whistleblowing 27 Â (1) Â Any person who has reasonable grounds to believe that a person has contravened or intends to contravene a provision of Division 1 or 1.1 may notify the Commissioner of the particulars of the matter and may request that their identity be kept confidential with respect to the notification. Marginal note: Confidentiality (2) Â The Commissioner shall keep confidential the identity of a person who has notified the Commissioner under subsection (1) and to whom an assurance of confidentiality has been provided by the Commissioner. 2000, c. 5, s. 27 2015, c. 32, s. 22 Previous Version Marginal note: Prohibition 27.1 Â (1) Â No employer shall dismiss, suspend, demote, discipline, harass or otherwise disadvantage an employee, or deny an employee a benefit of employment, by reason that (a) Â the employee, acting in good faith and on the basis of reasonable belief, has disclosed to the Commissioner that the employer or any other person has contravened or intends to contravene a provision of Division 1 or 1.1; (b) Â the employee, acting in good faith and on the basis of reasonable belief, has refused or stated an intention of refusin
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates secure storage and specific measures to protect confidential information. The changed text uses weaker language like 'endeavor,' 'reasonably secure manner,' and 'should consider,' making the obligation less strict. The Law Explanation states that PIPEDA Principle 4.7.3 requires organizations to protect personal information with security safeguards appropriate to the sensitivity of the information, and that the modified text might not require safeguards that are truly 'appropriate.' While neither scraped snippet directly addresses Principle 4.7.3, the Law Explanation combined with the weakening of the contractual language clearly indicates that the modified text creates a potentially non-compliant situation under the PIPEDA, thus creating a contradiction. The shift from a mandatory 'ensure' to a discretionary 'should consider' directly undermines the legal expectation of providing adequate safeguards, especially in light of potential sensitivity of the data being protected.

perturbation.original_text: You will only reproduce or take such copies of any of the Confidential Information as is reasonably necessary for the Permitted Purpose. You shall keep a record of the Confidential Information furnished to you, in any medium other than oral, and of the location of such Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: You may reproduce or take copies of the Confidential Information as deemed necessary. Maintaining a record of the Confidential Information furnished to you is at your discretion.
perturbation.explanation: The original text restricts copying to what is 'reasonably necessary' and mandates keeping a record. The modified text allows copying as 'deemed necessary,' which is subjective, and makes record-keeping discretionary, weakening the obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: PIPEDA requires organizations to implement procedures to protect personal information. Eliminating the mandatory record-keeping makes it harder to track and protect personal information, potentially leading to inadequate security safeguards, thereby contradicting PIPEDA Principle 4.7.3.

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates record-keeping of confidential information, but the modified text makes this discretionary. The Law Explanation states this weakening of record-keeping contradicts PIPEDA Principle 4.7.3, as it could lead to inadequate security safeguards. Although the provided scraped snippets do not explicitly mention Principle 4.7.3, they generally discuss promoting the protection of personal information and encouraging organizations to develop detailed policies and practices to comply with relevant divisions, which supports the argument that weakening record-keeping contradicts the intent of PIPEDA.

perturbation.original_text: The Potential Bidder will take all necessary precautions or measures as may be reasonable in the circumstances to prevent improper access to the Confidential Information or use or disclosure of the Confidential Information by the Potential Bidder’s Representatives and will be responsible and liable for any breach of this NDA by any of the Potential Bidder’s Representatives.
perturbation.changed_text: The Potential Bidder will consider taking precautions or measures to prevent improper access to the Confidential Information or use or disclosure of the Confidential Information by the Potential Bidder’s Representatives and may be responsible for breaches of this NDA by the Potential Bidder’s Representatives on a case-by-case basis.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will take all necessary precautions' to 'will consider taking precautions' weakens the obligation significantly. Also, making responsibility for breaches 'on a case-by-case basis' introduces uncertainty, reducing the deterrent effect and contradicting the strict liability intended by the original clause.
perturbation.law_explanation: PIPEDA holds organizations accountable for personal information under their control. By suggesting that responsibility for breaches by representatives will be determined 'on a case-by-case basis,' the modified text weakens the organization's accountability, potentially conflicting with PIPEDA's requirement to be responsible for personal information it possesses or controls.

perturbation.justification: The original text imposes a clear obligation on the Potential Bidder to 'take all necessary precautions,' while the changed text weakens this to 'consider taking precautions.' The original text also makes the Potential Bidder 'responsible and liable,' whereas the changed text suggests responsibility 'on a case-by-case basis.' This introduces ambiguity and weakens accountability for breaches, potentially conflicting with the principle that organizations are accountable for information under their control as highlighted in the LLM's Law Explanation. The lack of definitive legal snippets makes it harder to fully assess the scope of the violation but the altered text weakens accountability to the point of non-compliance with the spirit of the law.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that you or any of your Representatives become legally compelled or are required by regulatory authorities having appropriate jurisdiction to disclose any of the Confidential Information, you will promptly provide us with written notice so that we may seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy and/ or waive compliance with the provisions of this NDA. You will cooperate with us on a reasonable basis to obtain a protective order or other remedy.
perturbation.changed_text: If you or your Representatives believe you are legally compelled or required by regulatory authorities to disclose Confidential Information, you should consider notifying us. Cooperation to obtain a protective order will be at your discretion.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will promptly provide written notice' to 'should consider notifying us' makes notification optional. Also, stating that cooperation will be 'at your discretion' undermines the original text's intention of mandatory cooperation, weakening the protection of confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Alberta's Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act, Section 40(1)
perturbation.law_citation: Alberta's Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act, Section 40(1)
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 40(1) of Alberta's Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act requires organizations to protect confidential information of a third party. By making notification and cooperation discretionary, the organization may fail to take adequate steps to protect confidential information when legally compelled to disclose it, contradicting the spirit and intent of Alberta's FIPPA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act (Alberta) - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Alberta, Canada statute Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act Legislative Assembly of Alberta Citation Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act , R.S.O. 1990, c. F.31, s. 1 Territorial extent Province of Alberta Commenced June 1994 Status: In force The Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act , RSA 2000, c F-25 (FOIP Act) is the freedom of information and privacy act for Alberta , Canada . It was passed by the Alberta legislature in June 1994 and came into force on October 1, 1995. [ 1 ] Purpose [ edit ] "Purposes of this Act 2 The purposes of this Act are (a) to allow any person a right of access to the records in the custody or under the control of a public body subject to limited and specific exceptions as set out in this Act, (b) to control the manner in which a public body may collect personal information from individuals, to control the use that a public body may make of that information and to control the disclosure by a public body of that information, (c) to allow individuals, subject to limited and specific exceptions as set out in this Act, a right of access to personal information about themselves that is held by a public body, (d) to allow individuals a right to request corrections to personal information about themselves that is held by a public body, and (e) to provide for independent reviews of decisions made by public bodies under this Act and the resolution of complaints under this Act." — 1994 cF‑18.5 s2;1995 c17 s3 History [ edit ] In 2002, there were 62 changes made to the Act. [ 2 ] : i–vi This included amendments, references and potential changes to other acts such as the Traffic Safety Act, Vital Statistics Act, Election Act, Health Information Act, [ 3 ] Mines and Minerals Act, [ 4 ] Electronic Transactions Act, Occupational Health and Safety Act and the Municipal Government Act . The amendments recommended, that Alberta Energy "consider the protection of information provided in support of oil sands royalty calculations" the next time the Mines and Minerals Act is opened." [ 2 ] See also [ edit ] Freedom of information in Canada Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act Reference
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates providing written notice and cooperating to obtain a protective order, while the changed text makes notification discretionary ('should consider notifying us') and cooperation optional ('at your discretion'). The Law Explanation states that Section 40(1) of Alberta's Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act requires organizations to protect confidential information of a third party. The perturbation explanation confirms that making notification and cooperation discretionary undermines the protection of confidential information. Therefore, the modified text contradicts the original intent and creates a legal contradiction by potentially failing to protect confidential information as required by the Alberta FIPPA, as now the organization may not take sufficient steps to protect confidential information.
